text.fullText: Monday, February 16, 2026 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency EPA Docket Center Water Docket Mail Code 28221T 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Subject: Retain or Strengthen State & Tribal Clean Water Act Authority -- Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Document ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929-0001) To EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin: I strongly oppose the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations. The proposed rule’s regulatory changes would reduce the ability of states and tribes to evaluate the overall impacts of a federal infrastructure project on local waterways. Implementing this rule will upend decades of precedent, strip key powers from states and tribes, and dramatically undermine protections for clean water in the United States, putting drinking water sources, rivers, wetlands, and downstream communities at risk while making it easier for polluting projects to move forward without accountability. We don’t need to “streamline” fossil fuel infrastructure or new dams, especially at the expense of our waterways. “It is horrifying that we have to fight our own government to save the environment.” -- Ansel Adams This proposal comes immediately after Environmental Protection Agency’s rollback of federal water protections under the Waters of the United States. Together, these actions undermine the Clean Water Act’s purpose and threaten water quality, public health, and local economies. The Clean Water Act empowers states and tribes to examine how federal infrastructure projects, including pipelines, may impact local water quality. For 50 years, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act has provided essential protection for our nation’s waterways by giving states and tribes the important opportunity to weigh in on federally permitted projects and ensure the continued health of their waterways, environment, and people. When a state or tribe determines that an infrastructure project harms local waterways, they can leverage Section 401 of the Clean Water Act to reject infrastructure permits. This structure of cooperative federalism was central to the original design of the Clean Water Act. Every state is unique when it comes to the types of waterways it has, the ways in which those waterways support people and the economy and the challenges it faces in ensuring the continued health of those waterways. Because of this, states are in a better position than the federal government to understand the impacts of a proposed project and to come up with a plan to mitigate those impacts. “As we peer into society’s future, we—you and I, and our government—must avoid the impulse to live only for today, plundering for our own ease and convenience the Page 1 of 4 precious resources of tomorrow. We cannot mortgage the material assets of our grandchildren without risking the loss also of their political and spiritual heritage. We want democracy to survive for all generations to come, not to become the insolvent phantom of tomorrow.” -- Dwight D. Eisenhower The proposed rule would significantly limit Section 401 authority by restricting reviews to only “direct discharges” into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. The reduced scope of Section 401 under the proposed rule could result in damage to waterways other than through direct pollution. This change would weaken local communities’ ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources, thereby cutingt off drinking water, disrupting the migration of aquatic animals, and destroying natural floodplains. The proposed rule would also narrow the scope of tribal oversight by requiring that they evaluate projects through a separate authorization program. If finalized, this proposed rule would impose greater demands on tribes to prove that they have large-scale capacity to undergo a rigorous review of federal projects, even as much of the country grapples with restricted local government funding. Reducing the scope of the Clean Water Act’s Section 401 to only account for how direct discharges into waterways impact water quality will hinder the ability of state and tribal governments to fully consider how projects impact local communities and wildlife, with possible threats to public health, natural environments, and local economies. The proposed revisions would unlawfully and dangerously narrow the authority of states and tribes to protect their waters from harmful projects—especially large oil and gas pipelines. Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections. By limiting review to narrowly defined “discharges” and constraining timelines and conditions, the rule undermines Congress’s clear intent that states and tribes serve as frontline protectors of water quality. Section 401 ensures that local expertise and community voices are not overridden by federal permitting decisions. Weakening this authority would disproportionately harm our communities and watershed health and make it harder to prevent pollution before it occurs. “Our duty to the whole, including to the unborn generations, bids us to restrain an unprincipled present-day minority from wasting the heritage of these unborn generations. The movement for the conservation of wildlife and the larger movement for the conservation of all our natural resources are essentially democratic in spirit, purpose and method.” -- Theodore Roosevelt This rollback is especially concerning for the Great Lakes and the Appalachian regions. The Great Lakes hold 20% of the world’s surface fresh water and supply drinking water to more than 40 million people. In Michigan, the proposal could directly affect decisions regarding Enbridge’s Line 5 oil pipeline, including a proposed tunnel and potential horizontal directional drilling beneath the Straits of Mackinac—an area of extraordinary ecological, cultural, and economic significance. Page 2 of 4 Enbridge has delayed a final decision on its Clean Water Act Section 404 permit from the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy. If the Environmental Protection Agency finalizes this rule, it could affect the outcome by restricting Michigan’s ability—and tribal nations’ treaty-protected authority—to fully evaluate and reject water quality risks. “A thing is right when it tends to preserve the integrity, stability, and beauty of the biotic community. It is wrong when it tends otherwise.” -- Aldo Leopold Appalachian rivers contain incredible biodiversity, providing habitat for more than half of all aquatic species in North America, and this biodiversity supports local economies and livelihoods. Fishing is an important part of Appalachian culture, with many people depending on it as a source of food. Meanwhile, the biodiversity of Appalachia draws people from all over, who come to recreate and experience its beauty. These visitors help to support local economies, which often have limited other sources of income. One study estimated that outdoor recreation in western North Carolina generated $4.9 billion in economic output and created 48,000 jobs. However, these economic benefits depend upon clean and healthy waterways that can continue to support the region’s biodiversity. Appalachian waterways also provide drinking water to more than 36 million people. Keeping these source waters clean is essential to ensuring that these people continue to have access to safe and affordable water. If these waters become contaminated, municipal water systems will be forced to implement greater water treatment, and the increased costs associated with this treatment will be passed on to customers, many of whom are already struggling to afford high utility bills. For Appalachians who get their water from private wells or mountain springs, the impacts will be even more dire. Since they do not benefit from municipal water treatment, they will be forced to choose between drinking contaminated water or relying on prohibitively expensive alternatives, like bottled water. Healthy waterways also help to protect Appalachian communities from the most severe impacts of flooding. Heavy rain events are becoming increasingly common in Appalachia, resulting in deadly and damaging flooding events, like those associated with Hurricane Helene. Natural waterways help to slow down the flow of water, reducing risks to both people and property during heavy rains. However, projects that interfere with natural waterways and the flow of water can disrupt their ability to mitigate flooding, making these conditions more dangerous and more expensive for Appalachian communities. Section 401 of the Clean Water Act gives states the authority to protect their waterways from both discharges of pollutants and other related harms. Under the proposed rule, the states’ authority under this section would be dramatically narrowed, making it harder for states to ensure their waters are protected. For example, a state would not be able to impose conditions on a project that alters downstream flows of water, even though such a project could result in dangerous and costly flooding in downstream communities. A state would also be limited in its ability to regulate indirect impacts to water quality, such as those associated with project construction, despite the fact that these impacts could result in contaminated drinking water and increased water costs for residents. Page 3 of 4 “Every man who appreciates the majesty and beauty of the wilderness and of wild life, should strike hands with the farsighted men who wish to preserve our material resources, in the effort to keep our forests and our game beasts, game-birds, and game- fish—indeed, all the living creatures of prairie and woodland and seashore—from wanton destruction. Above all, we should realize that the effort toward this end is essentially a democratic movement.” -- Theodore Roosevelt Water is a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations. We cannot give away more of our waters to industry while our communities, health, wildlife, and environment pay the price. States and tribes must retain the power to: • Consider cumulative and long-term water quality impacts • Protect treaty rights, fisheries, and drinking water • Deny certification when projects threaten water quality standards I strongly urge you to reconsider this proposed rule. By limiting the ability of states and tribes to ensure the protection of their waters, the proposed rule threatens to damage the environment, hurt the economy, and increase costs for everyone. The Great Lakes, Appalachian rivers, and all of our fresh water sources are too important to risk for the convenience of oil pipeline companies. Our nation’s waterways deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental stewardship. The Environmental Protection Agency should withdraw this proposal and instead strengthen, not weaken, Clean Water Act protections. If it won’t strengthen protections, the Environmental Protection Agency should at least maintain Section 401 of the Clean Water Act as it is currently written. “The ultimate test of a moral society is the kind of world that it leaves to its children.” -- Dietrich Bonhoeffer Thank you for your consideration of my comments. Please do NOT add my name to your mailing list. I will learn about future developments on this issue from other sources. Sincerely, Christopher Lish San Rafael, CA Page 4 of 4
